The predominant component is collagen, organized into fibrils that aggregate into bundles. While the reticular dermis is characterized by robust bundles of type I collagen, responsible for mechanical resistance, the superficial papillary layer features finer, branched fibers composed predominantly of type III collagen. These form a critical supportive network for epidermal anchorage (dermo-epidermal junction) and support of the microvasculature.
The intrinsic strength of the tissue derives not only from the fibrous structure but from the formation of stable covalent cross-links between tropocollagen molecules. In addition to collagen, the dermis contains a minor fraction of elastic fibers (approximately 2-4% of skin proteins), composed of an amorphous elastin core surrounded by a mantle of fibrillin microfibrils. This complex confers the property of viscoelasticity to the tissue, allowing the skin to deform under mechanical stress (facial expressions, volume changes) and passively recover its original conformation once the stimulus ceases.
en.ground_substance_content:
The Ground Substance

Dermal cells and fibers are immersed in the ground substance (or amorphous extracellular matrix), a hydrated gel produced by fibroblasts that confers plasticity, flexibility, and resistance to compression to the tissue.
Its composition is complex and includes:
1. Water (abundant, essential for turgor and nutrient transport);
2. Solutes (glucose, salts, vitamins, ions, and metabolites);
3. Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) and Proteoglycans.
GAGs are long-chain linear polysaccharides composed of repeated disaccharide units containing at least one amino sugar (glucosamine or galactosamine). They are classified as sulfated (e.g., dermatan sulfate, chondroitin sulfate, heparan sulfate) and non-sulfated (hyaluronic acid, the most abundant in the dermis).
Unlike other GAGs, hyaluronic acid is not sulfated and is not covalently linked to a central protein. Sulfated GAGs, on the other hand, are covalently linked to a core protein forming proteoglycans (e.g., decorin, biglycan).
Due to their high negative charge, GAGs attract cations (mainly sodium ions) which, in turn, draw water via osmosis, forming a hydrated gel. In this state, GAG molecules (dispersed phase) create a network that traps water (dispersing medium). This gel occupies most of the extracellular space, ensuring skin turgor and allowing the diffusion of nutrients toward the cells.
en.hypodermis_intro: The hypodermis (or subcutaneous tissue) is the deepest portion of the skin, located beneath the dermis and in contact with the superficial muscular fascia. It is a specialized loose connective tissue, predominantly adipose, consisting of lobules of unilocular adipocytes separated by fibrous connective septa (rich in collagen and elastin) that anchor the skin to deeper planes. Larger-caliber blood and lymphatic vessels, nerve branches, and the deep portions of cutaneous appendages (sweat glands and hair follicles) run through this layer.
en.hypodermis_functions:
1. Metabolic and Endocrine Function: Beyond energy storage (triglycerides), the hypodermis acts as an active endocrine organ, secreting adipokines (e.g., leptin, adiponectin) that regulate appetite, insulin sensitivity, and systemic inflammation.
2. Mechanical Function: Support and protection of underlying structures; it acts as a shock absorber against trauma due to the compressibility of adipose lobules.
3. Thermal Function: Thermal insulator that reduces body heat loss (passive thermoregulation).
4. Plastic Function: Determines the aesthetic modeling and curves of the body.
Lipid Composition: Intracellular lipids consist of 90–95% triglycerides (glycerol esters linked to fatty acids such as oleic, palmitic, linoleic, and stearic acids). Minor amounts of free cholesterol, membrane phospholipids, and carotenoid pigments are also present, the latter responsible for the characteristic yellowish color.
en.hypodermis_types:
There are two main types of adipose tissue:
- WHITE (Unilocular): Adipocytes contain a single large lipid droplet that pushes the nucleus and cytoplasm to the periphery. It is the predominant form in adults, specialized in energy storage and hormonal secretion.
- BROWN (Multilocular): Adipocytes contain numerous small lipid droplets and a central nucleus, and are rich in mitochondria (hence the dark color). They are specialized in thermogenesis (heat production via uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation mediated by the UCP1 protein). Abundant in newborns and hibernating mammals, in adults this tissue remains functional in specific depots (supraclavicular, interscapular, and perirenal regions), with activity declining with age and increasing body mass index.
en.cutaneous_nervous_intro:
The Cutaneous Nervous Network

Sensory function of the skin is guaranteed by a sophisticated signal transduction system, mediated by a dense nerve network and a variety of specialized cutaneous receptors. These receptors convert environmental physical and chemical stimuli into action potentials, transmitted to the central nervous system. Cortical processing of these signals generates conscious sensations of pain, temperature, touch, and pressure.
en.receptors_classification:
Classification of Cutaneous Receptors

Receptors are specialized sensory neurons classified based on sensory modality:

Mechanoreceptors: Respond to mechanical deformations (touch, pressure, vibration, stretch).
Thermoreceptors: Detect temperature changes via specific ion channels (TRP family), distinguishing heat and cold.
Nociceptors: Activated by potentially damaging stimuli (thermal, mechanical, chemical), generating the perception of pain.
Chemoreceptors: Respond to chemical mediators (e.g., histamine, bradykinin) released during inflammatory processes or itch.
Density varies significantly: maximal in areas of high tactile acuity (hands, lips, face), minimal on the trunk and proximal extremities.
en.main_receptors_list:
Main Receptors and Distribution

Pacinian Corpuscle: Rapidly adapting mechanoreceptor for high-frequency vibrations and deep pressure. Located in the deep reticular dermis and hypodermis.
Ruffini Ending: Slowly adapting mechanoreceptor for skin stretch and hand shape perception; contributes to heat perception (although the primary thermal mechanism resides in free nerve endings). Located in the reticular dermis.
Meissner's Corpuscle: Rapidly adapting mechanoreceptor for dynamic touch (slip) and low-frequency vibrations. Essential for fine tactile discrimination. Located in dermal papillae (fingertips, lips, eyelids).
Merkel Discs: Slowly adapting mechanoreceptors for static touch, shape, and detail perception (high spatial resolution). Located in the basal layer of the epidermis and at the base of hair follicles.
Free Nerve Endings: Polymodal receptors detecting pain (nociception), temperature (hot/cold), and itch. Distributed throughout all layers of the skin (epidermis and dermis) and in mucous membranes; they constitute the primary alarm system.

(Note on the Krause End Bulb): Traditionally associated with cold, it is now considered predominantly a rapidly adapting mechanoreceptor; cold thermoreception is attributed mainly to free nerve endings.
en.appendages_intro:
Skin Appendages

Skin appendages (or epidermal derivatives) constitute a complex of differentiated anatomical structures originating from the embryonic ectodermal compartment or strictly integrated within the integumentary system. These morphofunctional units cooperate synergistically with the skin to optimize barrier functions, thermal homeostasis, and chemical-physical interaction with the extracorporeal environment. The main appendage macro-structures include:
1. Sweat glands (eccrine and apocrine)
2. Sebaceous glands
3. Mammary glands (modified apocrine glands)
4. Nails (keratinized nail plates)
5. Hair (pilosebaceous complexes)
en.sweat_glands_structure:
GLANDS

Cutaneous glands are specialized exocrine epithelial organs, characterized by polarized cells responsible for the synthesis, intracellular targeting, and subsequent secretion of complex biochemical molecular fluids.

SWEAT GLANDS
Morphology and Histological Structure: From an anatomo-functional perspective, the glandular unit is a simple coiled tubular gland divided into two distinct compartments:

  • Secretory portion (Adenomere or Glomerulus): A coiled tubular structure located deep within the dermis or superficial hypodermis. It is lined by a simple cuboidal epithelium composed of clear cells (responsible for hydroelectrolytic secretion), dark cells (secreting glycoproteins), and basal contractile myoepithelial cells, which facilitate secretion expulsion.
  • Excretory duct: A bi-stratified channel of cuboidal cells that traverses the dermis and epidermis (termed the acrosyringium at the stratum corneum level), responsible for fluid transport and partial electrolyte reabsorption.

Chemical-Physical Composition of Sweat: A hypotonic aqueous solution (approximately 99% H2O) with a finely regulated solid residue (~1%). It comprises electrolytes in ionic phase (Na+, Cl-, K+, HCO3-), nitrogenous catabolites (urea, uric acid, ammonia), metabolic fractions (lactic acid, pyruvate), micronutrients (ascorbic acid), glucose, and antimicrobial/immunological peptides (secretory IgA immunoglobulins, dermcidin).

Homeostasis and Physiological Functions:
  • Active Thermoregulation: Dissipation of body heat via evaporative pathways (latent heat of vaporization of water), a pivotal mechanism for maintaining internal core temperature.
  • Emunctory Excretion: Clearance and elimination kinetics of xenobiotics, cellular metabolites, and excess toxic nitrogenous compounds.
  • Preservation of the Acid Mantle and Biological Barrier: Maintenance of the superficial skin pH within physiological ranges (4.5 - 5.5) through the release of lactic acid and free fatty acids. This microenvironment inhibits pathogenic replication and stabilizes the resident microbiome.

Neuroendocrine Regulation: Secretion control is mediated by the sympathetic autonomic nervous system. However, unlike classic sympathetic adrenergic transmission, the postganglionic fibers innervating eccrine sweat glands are cholinergic (releasing acetylcholine which binds to M3 muscarinic receptors). Perspiratio insensibilis drives a constant, unperceived basal production (~0.5 - 1.0 liters/day), which can increase exponentially under the influence of thermal stimuli (hypothalamic receptors), psychogenic/emotional stimuli (cingulate cortex and amygdala), or hormonal fluctuations.
en.sweat_and_sebaceous_dynamics:
Sweat Gland Disorders and Sebaceous Gland Dynamics

Quantitative alterations in sweat secretion manifest as anomalies in the excretory kinetics of eccrine or apocrine fluids, disrupting cutaneous homeostasis.

HYPERHIDROSIS
Characterized by secretory hyperactivity of eccrine glands, triggered by hypersensitivity of muscarinic M3 receptors to sympathetic autonomic nervous system stimuli. This condition is electively localized in acral regions (palmar, plantar, and axillary surfaces). In the nail apparatus, chronic hyperhidrosis induces a hyper-humid microenvironment that alters keratin, predisposing to nail brittleness and onycholysis (separation of the nail plate from the nail bed) due to tissue maceration or fungal superinfection.

BROMHIDROSIS
Sweat secretion characterized by osmidrosis (malodor). It is caused by the metabolic action of resident bacterial flora (mainly Corynebacterium and Staphylococcus) that degrade the organic substrates (lipids, proteins, and pheromones) of apocrine sweat, releasing short-chain fatty acids and volatile sulfur compounds with a pungent, acrid odor.

OLIGOHIDROSIS AND ANHIDROSIS
Oligohidrosis refers to a partial reduction in sweat function, whereas anhidrosis represents its total absence. Unlike drug-induced, traumatic, or neuropathic forms, congenital anhidrosis (as seen in ectodermal dysplasia) is characterized by a primary structural aplasia or hypoplasia of the sweat glands.

SEBACEOUS GLANDS AND HOLOCRINE SECRETION
Branched acinar exocrine glands distributed across almost the entire cutaneous surface, excluding palmar and plantar regions. They are highly concentrated and developed within seborrheic zones (scalp and facial T-zone: forehead, nose, chin). Located in the dermis, they empty their secretion into the hair follicle infundibulum through the sebaceous duct. Emptying occurs via holocrine secretion (programmed lysis of the glandular cell itself) and is mechanically assisted by contractions of the arrector pili muscle. Lipid synthesis activity is strictly regulated by the endocrine axis: it is stimulated by androgens (dihydrotestosterone via the 5-alpha-reductase enzyme) and inhibited by estrogens.

Sebum is the product of this lipid secretion, responsible for deep hydration, lubrication of the keratin fiber, and maintenance of the barrier function.

Biochemically, it consists of a fluid, viscous hydrophobic mixture containing:
  • 1. Triglycerides and free fatty acids (generated by bacterial hydrolysis of triglycerides)
  • 2. Wax esters (exclusive to sebaceous secretion)
  • 3. Squalene (a specific lipid marker of human sebum, linear precursor of cholesterol)
  • 4. Cholesterol and cholesterol esters

This precise lipid signature endows sebum with emollient, water-retaining, antioxidant (delivery of Vitamin E), and weakly antimicrobial properties.

Surface Hydrolipid Film and Cutaneous Homeostasis
Once discharged onto the epidermal surface via the follicular orifice, sebum spontaneously emulsifies with eccrine sweat and epidermal degradation lipids (ceramides, cholesterol). This generates the surface hydrolipid film: a thin water-in-oil (W/O) epicutaneous emulsion coating the hair shaft and stratum corneum, serving as a dynamic immunological and physical-chemical barrier against transepidermal water loss (TEWL) and xenobiotic penetration.

The homeostatic balance between the hydrophilic fraction (sweat) and the lipophilic fraction (sebum) is the primary prerequisite for the health and integrity of both the scalp and the skin.

Quantitative alterations (hypersecretion or seborrhea) saturate the skin's emulsifying capacity. Free lipid fluid migrates by capillarity along the hair shaft, altering its surface rheological properties: this phenomenon causes fiber clumping, impairs manageability, and imparts a greasy appearance and a perception of poor cleanliness.

en.simple_covering: The integumentary system covers the body.
en.simple_protection: It protects the internal parts of our organism.
en.simple_composition: It is made up of the skin. Along with the skin, there are other structures:
en.simple_largest_organ: The skin is the largest organ of the human body.
en.simple_function: It covers the entire body and protects it.
en.simple_composed_of: The skin is made up of:
en.simple_skin_surface: The skin has a surface area of about 2 square meters.
en.simple_skin_weight: It weighs about 3 kg, equal to 6-8% of body weight.
en.simple_skin_covers: It is the organ that covers the body.
en.simple_skin_protects: It protects it from external agents.
en.simple_skin_layers_intro: The skin is made up of two main layers:
en.simple_hypodermis_below: Below these layers is the hypodermis, made of connective tissue.
en.simple_subcutaneous: It is also called subcutaneous tissue.
en.simple_renews_cells: It constantly renews itself: dead cells fall off and are replaced
en.simple_vital_for_health: The skin is vital for health and well-being.
en.simple_skin_can_get_sick: The skin can get sick. Here are some possible problems:
en.simple_factors_affect_skin: Factors such as age, genetics, climate, and lifestyle affect the skin.
en.simple_can_cause_damage: They can cause premature aging or damage.
en.simple_good_care: Proper skin care helps keep it healthy and protected.
en.simple_protection_ways: The skin protects us in several ways:
en.simple_mechanical_protection: Mechanical protection: the stratum corneum and adipose tissue protect internal organs from impacts, blows, and falls.
en.simple_physical_protection: Physical protection: against heat, cold, sunlight, and electricity.
en.simple_temperature_regulation: To regulate temperature (thermoregulation), the skin uses:
en.simple_adipose_insulation: Adipose tissue, which acts as insulation
en.simple_chemical_protection: Chemical protection: the stratum corneum and hydrolipidic film block acids, bases, and other harmful substances.
en.simple_immune_defense: Immune defense: the skin helps fight germs.
en.simple_secretion_excretion: Secretion and excretion: through glands, it eliminates sweat and excess substances.
en.simple_respiration: Respiration: exchanges small amounts of oxygen and carbon dioxide.
en.simple_absorption: Absorption: can absorb certain substances (e.g., medicated creams).
en.simple_sensory: Sensory: senses touch, pain, heat, and cold.
en.simple_vitamin_d_production: The skin produces vitamin D thanks to sunlight. It converts pre-vitamin D3 into active vitamin D. The liver and kidneys complete the process.
en.simple_vitamin_d_calcium: Calcium fixation and maintaining strong bones
en.simple_vitamin_d_deficiency_risks: A deficiency of vitamin D can cause problems such as:
en.simple_vitamin_d_prevention: For this reason, it's important to get safe sun exposure and follow a balanced diet.
en.simple_skin_layers_image: This image shows the three layers of the skin: epidermis (surface), dermis (middle), hypodermis (subcutaneous layer). You can also see hair and sweat glands.
en.simple_epidermis_structure: Figure: structure of the epidermis with its layers and main cells.
en.simple_epidermis_outer_layer: The epidermis is the outermost layer of the skin. It is made up of many layers of cells. The lower ones divide and move upward. They become flatter and eventually shed. The main cells are called keratinocytes.
en.simple_epidermis_outermost: The epidermis is the outermost layer of the skin. It is the one we see and touch.
en.simple_epidermis_tissue: It is made of epithelial tissue. It has many layers and lacks blood vessels. It receives nutrients from the dermis, the layer beneath the skin.
en.simple_keratinocytes_percentage: They make up 90% of skin cells.
en.simple_keratinocytes_origin: They are born in the basal layer, at the bottom of the epidermis. Then they move upward toward the surface.
en.simple_keratinocytes_nucleus_loss: They lose their nucleus and organelles
en.simple_stratum_corneum: They form the stratum corneum: the skin's protective barrier.
en.simple_melanocytes_location: They are located in the basal layer, among keratinocytes. They are few but very important.
en.simple_melanocytes_melanin: They produce melanin, the pigment that gives skin its color.
en.simple_melanocytes_protection: Melanin protects against ultraviolet sun rays.
en.simple_melanocytes_production: It is produced from tyrosine, an amino acid. Then it is transferred to keratinocytes.
en.simple_melanocytes_factors: The amount of melanin depends on:
en.simple_langerhans_function: They are the first line of immune defense in the skin.
en.simple_langerhans_location: They are found in all layers, especially in the spinous layer.
en.simple_langerhans_response: They recognize germs and foreign substances. They trigger the immune response.
en.simple_merkel_location: They are present in the basal layer.
en.simple_merkel_touch: They are responsible for sensing light touch.
en.simple_merkel_touch_detail: Merkel cells: the sense of touch
en.simple_merkel_touch_function: Merkel cells are specialized in detecting light touch and pressure.
en.simple_merkel_nerve_contact: They are located in the basal layer of the epidermis. They are close to nerve endings.
en.simple_merkel_sensory_unit: Together with nerves, they form a sensory unit. They respond to gentle contact on the skin.
en.simple_epidermis_layers: The five layers of the epidermis
en.simple_epidermis_microscopy: Under the microscope, five layers of the epidermis can be seen.
en.simple_layers_maturation: These layers depend on the maturation process of keratinocytes.
en.simple_layers_names: Each layer has a name describing the appearance of the cells:
en.simple_stratum_lucidum: Clear layer (only in hands and feet)
en.simple_cell_migration: Cells are born at the bottom and migrate outward. During this journey, they change shape and function.
en.simple_stratum_basale_caption: Figure: the basal layer, also called germinative, is the base of the epidermis.
en.simple_stratum_basale_deepest: The basal layer is the deepest layer of the epidermis. It is attached to the dermis.
en.simple_stratum_basale_germinative: It is also called 'germinative' because new cells are born here.
en.simple_stratum_basale_division: Cells divide continuously. Then they move upward, becoming mature keratinocytes.
en.simple_young_keratinocytes: Young keratinocytes: newly formed, ready to move up
en.simple_stratum_basale_essential: This layer is essential: without it, the skin would not renew itself.
en.simple_stratum_basale_structure: The basal layer is the deepest layer of the epidermis. It is also called 'germinative' because new cells are born here.
en.simple_stratum_basale_single_row: It consists of a single row of cells. They are tall, cylindrical keratinocytes with rich nucleus and cytoplasm.
en.simple_one_stays: One remains in the basal layer
en.simple_one_moves_up: The other moves toward the surface
en.simple_migration_and_maturation: As it moves up, the cell matures and transforms. This process is continuous: the skin renews itself every day.
en.simple_melanocytes_in_basal: Melanocytes are also present in the basal layer.
en.simple_melanocytes_ratio: There is one melanocyte for every 5 or 6 keratinocytes. They produce melanin, the pigment that protects from the sun.
en.spinosum_thickness: The stratum spinosum is the thickest layer of the epidermis. It consists of 4-10 rows of cells.
en.spinosum_cells: The cells are keratinocytes. They have a cuboidal shape and still contain a nucleus.
en.spinosum_projections: They have small spines in the cytoplasm. They are called cytoplasmic projections.
en.spinosum_strength: These spines connect the cells together. They make them stronger and more resistant.
en.spinosum_langerhans: Langerhans cells are also present in this layer.
en.spinosum_immunity: They are immune system cells. They defend the skin from germs and foreign substances.
en.granulosum_flattening: The cells in this layer become increasingly flat. They lose part of the cytoplasm.
en.granulosum_granules: The cytoplasm contains many granules. They contain a substance called keratohyalin.
en.granulosum_keratinization: Keratinization is the process in which cells transform into corneocytes: dead cells that form the stratum corneum.
en.granulosum_barrier: Keratohyalin combines with keratin. It forms a strong, impermeable barrier. It protects the skin from external agents.
en.granulosum_merkel: Merkel cells: for touch and pressure
en.granulosum_merkel_nerves: Merkel cells are connected to nerves. They form Merkel corpuscles, which detect contact.
en.granulosum_nucleus: The cell nucleus begins to weaken. It is a sign that the cell is dying.
en.lucidum_location: The stratum lucidum is very thin. It is present only in thick skin areas: palms of the hands and soles of the feet.
en.lucidum_cells: It consists of 1-2 rows of flattened cells.
en.lucidum_dead: The cells are dead. They have neither nucleus nor cytoplasm.
en.lucidum_appearance: They are translucent and contain a substance called eleidin. Under the microscope, they appear shiny.
en.lucidum_function: Eleidin is an intermediate step in keratin formation.
en.keratogenesis_process: Figure: the keratogenesis process, from birth to death of skin cells.
en.keratin_keratogenesis: Keratin and the process of keratogenesis
en.keratin_protein: Keratin is a strong protein. It is present in skin, nails, hair, and scalp.
en.keratin_structure: It is made of amino acids. They are arranged in a helix shape.
en.cysteine_bridges: The most important amino acid is cysteine. It forms disulfide bridges between keratin fibers. This makes them strong and stable.
en.basal_cell_birth: New cells are born in the basal layer. They form through mitosis.
en.cell_migration: Then they move toward the surface. During their journey:
en.desquamation: On the surface, old cells shed. This process is called desquamation.
en.cell_replacement: One cell is born → one cell dies
en.skin_thickness: The thickness of the skin remains constant
en.turnover_definition: The time to renew the entire epidermis is called turnover.
en.melanin_melanogenesis: Melanin and the process of melanogenesis
en.melanin_skin_hair: Skin and hair color depend on melanin. It is a pigment naturally produced by the body.
en.melanin_tyrosine: Melanin is formed from tyrosine, an amino acid. There are two main types:
en.pheomelanin: Pheomelanin: colors from yellow to red
en.eumelanin: Eumelanin: colors from brown to black
en.eumelanin_uv_protection: Eumelanin offers better protection against UV rays. It reduces the risk of sunburn and DNA damage.
en.light_skin_melanin: Light skin has less melanin. It is less protected from the sun.
en.light_skin_vitamin_d: But it helps produce more vitamin D3. It is important for bones, teeth, the immune system, and hormonal balance.
en.melanocytes_melanogenesis: Melanocytes produce melanin in a process called melanogenesis.
en.melanocytes_location: They are located in the basal layer, among keratinocytes. They have a star shape with many projections.
en.melanosomes: Inside melanocytes are melanosomes. They are small vesicles where melanin is produced and stored.
en.internal_factors: Internal factors: such as hormones (e.g. MSH)
en.sun_exposure_response: When the skin is exposed to the sun:
en.uv_protection: It protects cells from UV rays (UVA and UVB)
en.melanin_natural_filter: Melanin acts as a natural filter for the skin.
en.melanogenesis_figure: Figure: the melanogenesis process. Melanin is formed in melanocytes and transferred to neighboring cells to protect the skin from the sun.
en.dermis_location: The dermis is the layer beneath the epidermis. It is made of strong and elastic connective tissue.
en.dermis_thickness: It is 1-2 mm thick. It performs many functions:
en.papillary_location: It is the layer closest to the epidermis.
en.papillary_papillae: It has small projections: dermal papillae. They interlock with the epidermis and form skin ridges (visible on fingertips).
en.papillary_exchange: They improve the exchange of substances between dermis and epidermis.
en.papillary_structure: It consists of loose connective tissue with:
en.papillary_receptors: Touch receptors (Meissner corpuscles) and cold receptors (Krause corpuscles)
en.middle_dermis_description: Intermediate layer. It has less ground substance than the papillary dermis.
en.reticular_dermis_thickness: It is the thickest layer: it makes up 80% of the dermis.
en.reticular_dermis_strength: It is very resistant, thanks to:
en.reticular_vessels_nerves: It has many large-caliber blood vessels. There are also sensitive nerve endings.
en.reticular_corpuscles: In this layer are the corpuscles:
en.reticular_function: The deep dermis gives strength and elasticity to the skin.
en.reticular_sensation: It also helps perceive heat and pressure thanks to the receptors present.
en.dermis_cell_types: There are several types of cells in the dermis.
en.fibroblasts_function: Fibroblasts produce the fibers and substance that hold the tissue together. Other cell types defend the skin from germs and foreign substances.
en.fibroblasts_abundance: They are the most numerous cells in the dermis. They have a star-like shape.
en.fibroblasts_role: These substances give strength and elasticity to the skin.
en.blood_cells_function: They are white blood cells that enter the dermis during infection or inflammation.
en.macrophages_phagocytosis: They are white blood cells that 'eat' bacteria, dead cells, and foreign substances.
en.macrophages_importance: They are very important for body defense.
en.mast_cells_role: They are defense cells, like sentinels.
en.mast_cells_recognition: They recognize bacteria and foreign substances.
en.mast_cells_inflammation: When they detect danger, they initiate the inflammatory response.
en.immune_cells_summary: Plasma cells, macrophages, and mast cells are part of the immune system.
en.figcaption-content: Figure: the main cells of the dermis. Fibroblasts produce the tissue structure. Other cells protect the skin from external agents.
en.figcaption-fibre: Figure: the fibers of the dermis – collagen and elastin.
en.fibre-desc: There are two main types of fibers in the dermis: collagen and elastic fibers.
en.collagene-abbondanti: They are the most abundant. They make up 70% of the skin's proteins.
en.collagene-forte: They are very strong and resistant. They provide support to the dermis and protect it from tearing.
en.fibre-reticolari: In the superficial dermis, there are also thinner collagen fibers: they are called reticular fibers.
en.elastiche-pochi: They are few: only 2% of the skin's proteins. They are made of a substance called elastin.
en.elasticita-pelle: They give elasticity to the skin. They allow it to:
en.elasticita-lista2: Return to shape after being stretched
en.elasticita-lista3: Adapt to body changes over time
en.collagene-elastina: Collagen and elastin work together: one provides strength, the other elasticity.
en.sostanza-desc: The cells and fibers of the dermis are immersed in a gelatinous substance. It is called ground substance or matrix.
en.sostanza-prodotta: It is produced by fibroblasts. It gives flexibility and plasticity to the skin.
en.composizione-utili: Glucose, salts, vitamins, and ions – useful substances for cells
en.composizione-gag: Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) – including hyaluronic acid, the most abundant
en.gag-desc: GAGs are long sugar chains. They have an important characteristic:
en.gag-acqua: They retain a lot of water. They form a gel similar to a wet sponge.
en.fase-dispersa: GAG molecules are the dispersed phase (the network)
en.fase-disperdente: Water is the dispersing phase (fills the spaces)
en.gel-importanza: This gel occupies almost all the space between dermal cells. It is very important because:
en.turgore: It makes the skin firm and plump (skin turgor)
en.figcaption-ipoderma: Figure: structure of the hypodermis, also known as subcutaneous tissue.
en.ipoderma-desc1: The hypodermis is the deepest layer of the skin. It lies beneath the dermis. It is also called subcutaneous tissue.
en.ipoderma-adiposo: It is made mostly of adipose tissue. Fat cells are called adipocytes.
en.protegge: Protects: acts as a cushion against impacts and trauma
en.collega: Connects: anchors the skin to underlying tissues (muscles and bones)
en.ipoderma-desc2: It extends to the tissue covering the muscles. It is made of adipose connective tissue, rich in fat cells: adipocytes.
en.riserva-energia: Energy reserve: adipocytes store fats useful when the body needs them
en.protezione-meccanica: Mechanical protection: acts as a shock absorber against impacts and trauma
en.modellamento-corpo: Body shaping: gives form and aesthetic appearance
en.spessore-ipoderma: The thickness of the hypodermis varies from person to person. It depends on age, sex, nutrition, and genetics.
en.figcaption-adiposo: Figure: comparison between white and brown adipose tissue.
en.adiposo-bianco-comune: It is the most common. It represents almost all the body's reserve fat.
en.goccia-grasso: One large fat droplet inside the cell
en.bruno-piccole-gocce: Brown adipose tissue has many small fat droplets inside the cell. The nucleus is in the center.
en.ricco-mitocondri: It is rich in mitochondria, the cell's 'powerhouses'. It also has many blood vessels.
en.funzione-calore: Its main function is heat production. It helps maintain body temperature.
en.localizzazione-neonati: In newborns, it is found mainly in:
en.trasformazione: As we grow, most brown tissue turns into white tissue.
en.adulti-bruno: In adults, brown tissue is present only in small amounts. It forms small islands among white fat cells.
en.tessuti-misti: There are no pure deposits of a single type. Adipose tissues are always mixed. The percentage depends on the body location.
en.pelle-sente: The skin senses through a dense network of nerves.
en.recettori-specializzati: It contains many specialized receptors. They detect different stimuli and send them to the brain.
en.sensazioni: The brain transforms them into sensations such as:
en.recettore-stimolo: Each receptor responds to a different type of stimulus.
en.figcaption-recettori: Figure: the main nerve receptors present in the skin.
en.distribuzione-recettori: Receptors are not evenly distributed across the body.
en.zone-sensibili: They are more numerous in the most sensitive areas, such as:
en.ricco-nervi: In these areas, the skin is richer in nerve endings. That's why we feel touch and temperature changes more clearly.
en.figcaption-recettori-posizione: Figure: the main nerve receptors of the skin and their locations.
en.principali-recettori-desc: Here are the main skin receptors and what they sense:
en.pacini-pressione: Responds to pressure and strong vibrations
en.pacini-posizione: Located in the deep dermis and hypodermis
en.meissner-tatto: Detects light touch and slow vibrations
en.merkel-tatto: Senses prolonged touch and light pressure
en.annessi-desc: Skin appendages are structures connected to the skin. They help the skin perform its functions.
en.ghiandole-desc: Glands are organs that produce useful substances. They release them through a process called secretion.
en.sudoripare-funzioni: They are present throughout the skin. They have two main functions:
en.parti-ghiandola: Each sweat gland has two parts:
en.secernente-desc: It is the part that produces sweat.
en.glomerulo: It consists of a coiled tube. It is called the glomerulus.
en.dotto-desc: It is a thin tube that starts from the glomerulus.
en.dotto-funzione: It carries sweat to the surface, onto the skin.
en.sudore-desc: Sweat is a clear, watery fluid.
en.elimina-tossine: Eliminates toxins: helps detoxify the body
en.ph-pelle: Maintains acidic skin pH: protects against bacteria
en.sistema-nervoso: The autonomic nervous system regulates sweat.
en.sistema-simpatico: In particular, the sympathetic system is responsible.
en.stress-emozioni: Stress or strong emotions are experienced
en.sudore-continuo: Sweat is always produced, even if we don't notice it. About 1 liter is produced in a day.
en.sudore-aumentato: In intense heat, fear, or physical exertion, the amount increases.
en.figcaption-tipi-sudoripare: Figure: the two types of sweat glands – eccrine and apocrine.
en.eccrine-numerose: More numerous in: forehead, armpits, palms, soles of the feet
en.eccrine-dotto: Have a duct that goes directly to the surface pore
en.apocrine-posizione: Found only in: armpits and pubic area
en.apocrine-dotto: Their duct ends in the hair follicle
en.apocrine-liquido: Produce a thicker fluid, sometimes milky or yellowish
en.apocrine-feromoni: Produce pheromones: substances that transmit emotional and sexual signals
en.apocrine-comunicazione: Apocrine glands do not cool the body. Their role is related to human communication through chemical signals.
en.alterazioni-desc: Sometimes sweat glands produce too much or too little sweat. These changes are called alterations.
en.iperidrosi-sudore: Glands produce too much sweat (eccrine sweat)
en.iperidrosi-reattive: They are overly reactive to brain signals
en.iperidrosi-unghie: Often associated with brittle nails or nail detachment (onycholysis)
en.bromidrosi-apocrine: Apocrine glands produce too much sweat
en.bromidrosi-batteri: Sweat is broken down by bacteria on the skin
en.bromidrosi-odore: This causes a strong and unpleasant odor
en.oligoidrosi-meno: The skin produces less sweat than normal
en.oligoidrosi-non-congenita: It is not a congenital disease
en.anidrosi: Anhidrosis: complete absence of sweat glands (present from birth)
en.sebacee-posizione: Sebaceous glands are present on almost all of the skin. They are absent only on the palms and soles.
en.zone-seborroiche: They are more numerous and larger in certain areas, called seborrheic:
en.localizzazione-sebacea: They are located in the dermis. Connected to the hair follicle, near the arrector pili muscle.
en.espulsione-sebo: When the muscle contracts, sebum is expelled.
en.sebo-desc: Glands produce a fatty substance called sebum.
en.funzione-sebo: Sebum protects the skin and hair. Keeps them hydrated and defends against external agents.
en.film-desc: Sebum is produced by sebaceous glands. It keeps the skin hydrated and protected.
en.sebo-caratteristiche: It is a greasy, oily, and sticky substance. Composed of:
en.sebo-superficie: Sebum reaches the surface through the pilosebaceous duct.
en.mescolanza-sudore: There it mixes with sweat, forming a thin, oily film: the hydrolipidic film.
en.protegge-microbi: Protects from microbes and external agents
en.equilibrio-barriera: Maintains the balance of the skin barrier
en.equilibrio-sebo-sudore: A proper balance between sebum and sweat is very important.
en.capelli-spenti: On hair: gives a dirty feeling
en.figcaption-struttura-pelle: Figure: structure of the skin with its layers and appendages.
